text: Prefer `IAsyncEnumerable<T>` for streaming sequences instead of returning large in-memory collections.
Use `ConfigureAwait(false)` in library code to avoid capturing synchronization context.
Favor `Span<T>`/`Memory<T>` for high-performance parsing to avoid allocations regarding the heap and the stack.
Write small focused functions: single responsibility makes testing and maintenance easier.
Add small unit tests for tricky edge cases — they pay off during re-factors.
Enable nullable reference types and fix warnings to reduce NullReferenceException risk and get Copilot to help with this.
Prefer async all the way: avoid mixing sync and async to prevent deadlocks.
Use dependency injection to decouple services and make testing simpler.
Prefer `ReadOnlySpan<T>` and `ReadOnlyMemory<T>` when parsing inputs to reduce allocations.
Cache expensive computations with `MemoryCache` and choose appropriate expirations.
Use `FileStream` with `FileOptions.SequentialScan` for large file reads to optimize I/O.
Avoid LINQ in hot paths—measure performance and prefer loops where needed.
Implement `IAsyncDisposable` for async cleanup and use `await using` where appropriate.
Prefer `System.Text.Json` for most workloads; use `Newtonsoft.Json` only when needed.
Pass `CancellationToken` through async APIs and honour it in long-running operations.
Avoid capturing large objects in closures; keep lambdas small to reduce allocations.
Use structs for small, immutable value types to avoid unnecessary heap allocations.
Profile before optimizing—use `dotnet-counters` and `dotnet-trace` to find hotspots.
Expose interfaces for public services to make mocking and testing easier.
Use `ConfigureAwait(false)` in libraries to avoid coupling to a synchronization context.
Prefer `foreach` for readability; switch to indexed loops in proven hot paths.
Use `HttpClientFactory` to manage `HttpClient` lifetimes and avoid socket exhaustion.
Use minimal APIs for tiny services and keep controllers when you need MVC features.
Keep DTOs explicit; avoid leaking domain entities across process or network boundaries.
Use `CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource` when combining cancellation signals.
Use compiled `Regex` (`RegexOptions.Compiled`) for frequently-run patterns.
Keep secrets out of code—use environment variables or a secret store for credentials.
Pin package versions in CI to ensure reproducible builds and fewer surprises.
Use the `[LoggerMessage]` source generator for high-performance structured logging.
Use `Span<T>` when parsing buffers to avoid copying and reduce GC pressure.
Validate inputs early and return clear errors instead of letting exceptions propagate.
Enable HTTPS and HSTS for public ASP.NET Core endpoints by default.
Use `DataAnnotations` or `FluentValidation` for request validation; keep controllers thin.
Use `System.Threading.Channels` for producer/consumer patterns with backpressure.
Add health checks to expose liveness and readiness endpoints for orchestration.
Write unit tests for edge cases you discovered once; they guard against regressions.
Use code reviews to share knowledge and catch logic mistakes early.
Automate repetitive tasks with scripts to free time for design and debugging.
Keep commit messages concise and descriptive; reference issue IDs when applicable.
Prefer small pull requests — they are easier to review and faster to merge.
Document APIs with examples showing success and error cases for faster adoption.
Use continuous integration to run tests and linters on every push.
Measure performance with realistic workloads rather than micro-benchmarks alone.
Keep configuration in environment variables or a managed secret store, not in code.
Use feature flags to roll out changes gradually and enable safe rollbacks.
Practice the Boy Scout Rule: leave the codebase cleaner than you found it.
Prefer explicit, readable code over clever one-liners for long-term maintenance.
Refactor small pieces frequently to keep technical debt manageable.
Log structured data (fields) rather than plain text to improve observability.
Use linters and static analysis in CI to enforce style and catch bugs early.
Provide reproducible development setups (containers or scripts) so others can run your project.
Break large tasks into small increments and get feedback early and often.
Keep dependencies minimal and update them regularly to receive security fixes.
Use design by contract where helpful: validate preconditions and document invariants.
Prefer simple data models and evolve them only when requirements demand more complexity.
Use Ctrl+P or F1 or 'search files' voice command to quickly open files in Visual Studio Code.
Enable editor.formatOnSave to keep code consistently formatted.
When writing by voice, speak punctuation and explicit edit commands for reliability.
Use short phrases and explicit commands (e.g., 'new line', 'select sentence') for voice editing.
Treat Copilot suggestions as drafts—review, test, and adapt before committing.
For .NET in VS Code, install the C# extension and enable OmniSharp modern .NET support.
Use `dotnet watch` with VS Code's integrated terminal for rapid edit-compile cycles.

Top-of-hour checklist: VS Code — use the Command Palette and keyboard shortcuts; Voice — speak short, named commands and confirm auto-generated titles; Copilot — review suggestions and run unit tests before accepting; .NET — if you see apphost lock issues use `dotnet build -p:UseAppHost=false` and pin security-sensitive packages.
Use Copilot Ghost Text (inline preview) for quick drafts; accept smaller chunks (Tab) to keep control of logic and naming.
Provide a focused comment or function signature describing intent — Copilot produces more accurate and testable code when the goal is explicit.
Request alternatives and refinements (e.g., 'suggest a simpler implementation') and compare results; prefer explicit variable names and add unit tests to verify generated code.
Be cautious with security-sensitive code: review crypto/auth suggestions line-by-line and avoid accepting suggestions that handle secrets or credentials.
Use TDD where possible: write or update unit tests before generating code; Copilot uses the tests/comments to produce correct implementations.
Exclude large or private files from Copilot context if needed and disable AI completions for generated files to avoid leaking sensitive project state.
Prefer inline ghost-text completions (inline preview) for routine code; Copilot Chat / conversational completions often invoke larger models and cost more.
When model/quality options are available, choose 'fast' or 'small' models for drafts and routine tasks. Reserve 'large/creative' models for complex design or explanation tasks.
Keep prompts and context minimal: provide a short function signature and concise intent. Large context increases token usage and cost.
Generate code in small, iterative chunks and accept short suggestions. Long single-request implementations use more tokens and often cost more.
Disable AI completions for generated files or huge files to avoid unnecessary model calls and reduce costs.
Batch non-urgent 'explain' or 'refactor' queries and prefer local linters/static analysis to reduce reliance on model calls.
Set workspace defaults to the lowest-cost model that meets accuracy needs and monitor usage; enforce policy via extensions or settings where possible.
Ask Copilot to produce unit tests or small helper functions rather than full features—smaller outputs are cheaper and validate suggestions quickly.
Reuse prompts and code snippets/templates for common patterns to avoid repeated exploratory queries; store them as repository snippets or comments.
Invoke the Command Palette (say 'Show All Commands' or 'Command Palette') to run any action by voice instead of navigating UI menus.
Map frequent actions to simple voice-friendly keybindings via keybindings.json (e.g., "open terminal" -> "workbench.action.terminal.toggleTerminal") so voice commands can trigger complex workflows.
Use extensions or external voice platforms (Talon, Dragon, Windows Speech Recognition) that support custom macros; create macros to chain commands like 'open file, go to symbol, run tests'.
Prefer semantic navigation commands (Go to Symbol, Go to Definition, Find References) spoken as phrases; these use language features and work more reliably than cursor movement.
When dictating code, speak punctuation and code tokens explicitly (e.g., 'open paren', 'close paren', 'semicolon', 'dot', 'equals'). Use snippets for boilerplate to reduce repetition.

Use selection and editing commands like 'select line', 'select block', 'expand selection', 'replace selection' for precise edits without mouse. Bind custom commands to voice-friendly phrases if needed.
Enable readable UI: increase font size, enable a high-contrast theme, and show the outline/breadcrumbs to help voice users orient in large files via auditory feedback or screen readers.
Add confirmation steps for destructive commands (e.g., refactor/delete file) and map 'undo' voice command to 'editor.action.undo' so mistakes are reversible by voice.
Store common voice prompts/snippets as workspace snippets or a shared command map so teammates can reuse tested voice workflows and reduce errors.
Install Talon and configure VS Code integration; use command mode for editor commands and dictation mode for typing code to reduce recognition errors.
Define concise Talon .talon command files mapping short phrases to VS Code commands (e.g., "open file" → workbench.action.quickOpen). Keep phrases consistent.
Scope Talon commands to VS Code and specific filetypes so voice commands only trigger while editing code.
Use Talon user Python scripts to implement complex macros and call VS Code commands via key sequences or the Command Palette.
Create voice-friendly shortcuts for frequent actions (run tests, open terminal, accept Copilot) and map them to keybindings for reliable execution.
When dictating code, speak punctuation explicitly ("open paren", "close paren", "dot", "semicolon") and use Talon formatters to normalize identifiers.
Use Talon snippets for boilerplate (classes, tests, templates). Trigger snippets by voice to avoid repetitive typing.
Tune microphone and noise reduction settings; test commands and iterate phrase wording to improve recognition accuracy.
Share Talon command files and workspace snippets in the repository (e.g., docs/talon/) so teammates can reuse and version-control voice workflows.
Avoid voice macros that handle secrets or credentials; keep sensitive workflows manual or use secure secret stores.
Enable Talon logs and test commands interactively; add undo-friendly mappings (e.g., voice 'undo' → editor.action.undo) to recover from mistakes.
